children.value: Copperline can run as a headless GDB remote target:

. Use an explicit
address such as

only on a trusted network: the remote
protocol can read and write guest RAM and can resume the emulated machine.
Connect from GDB with the 68k architecture selected:

The target starts paused at reset. The stub implements the normal all-stop
remote packets for register access, RAM reads/writes, hardware-style PC
breakpoints, memory watchpoints, single-step, continue, Ctrl-C interrupt,
program load events (

). Detaching leaves the machine
paused and the stub listening, so a later

picks up the
same session (and re-runs
"monitor" command not supported by this target.

, GDB is not actually connected: that is GDB’s built-in
dummy target speaking after a failed

scripts carry on past the connection error. A common cause is running
GDB inside a container (e.g. a cross-toolchain Docker image): the
container’s

is not the host’s, so it never reaches
Copperline’s

listener. Run GDB on the host, or bind an
address the container can reach (

, trusted networks
only per the warning above) and connect to the host’s address.
GDB sees the core 68000 register set as

. Register writes go through Copperline’s CPU wrapper so SR stack
banking and interrupt state stay coherent.
Generic GDB memory packets are intentionally conservative:

Reads use a side-effect-free CPU-visible RAM/ROM view, including the boot
ROM overlay.

Writes modify RAM-backed regions only: chip RAM, trapdoor slow RAM, and
configured RAM expansion boards.

Writes to ROM, overlay ROM, custom chips, CIA, RTC, IDE, SCSI, CD, and
other device windows are ignored by
The stub is exercised against real clients:

(the
bebbo amiga-gcc toolchain) connects, disassembles live ROM, steps,
breaks, and reverse-steps as expected. A stock multi-arch host

also accepts the target description, but without an executable loaded
it guesses endianness from the host -- issue
(or just load your program’s ELF with

, which the
Amiga toolchains’ gdb does implicitly).

(gdb) monitor status
(gdb) monitor beam
(gdb) monitor custom
(gdb) monitor reg DMACON
(gdb) monitor reg DFF100
(gdb) monitor write-reg COLOR00 00f

Custom-register inspection is side-effect-free. It reads Copperline’s
internal Agnus/Denise/Paula/blitter latches rather than executing a real CPU
read from

, so it will not acknowledge interrupts, clear latches, or
advance collision/audio state.

is different: it routes a word
write through the normal custom-register write path and therefore has real
hardware effects.
Register names match the debugger window (

then stops when the Agnus beam reaches that
position, at exact colour-clock granularity, even while the CPU sits in

omitted means the start of the line, and a trap re-fires
every frame until removed.

(gdb) monitor beam-trap 100
(gdb) monitor beam-trap 100 40
(gdb) monitor clear-beam-traps

stops when the Copper’s PC arrives at that list address, before the
instruction there executes.

The Copper list can be dumped from the live list pointer, the current Copper
PC, or an explicit chip-RAM address:

(gdb) monitor copper
(gdb) monitor copper pc 20
(gdb) monitor copper 00c01000 80

Counts are hexadecimal, matching GDB’s packet syntax and Copperline’s other
debugger address inputs.

arms the same snapshot-ring reverse debugger used by the window and
headless reverse watchpoint. GDB commands map as follows:
run backward to the previous GDB PC breakpoint
find the last instruction that changed the watched word

, with the same tradeoff as the other frontends:
more memory and more frequent snapshots make reverse operations faster.
For byte-identical replay, keep the usual determinism requirements from

when guest RTC reads matter, and
avoid externally mutating hard-drive/CD images during a debug session.

What symbol detail you get depends on the file you feed GDB, not on the
stub:
An AmigaOS hunk executable built with

(bebbo’s amiga-gcc) carries
a hunk symbol table:
, named disassembly -- but no DWARF, so source lines,

on variables are unavailable. Reading hunk files
at all requires a GDB whose BFD includes the

); some builds of the same toolchain
lack it and report “file format not recognized”.

Full source-level debugging needs an ELF file with DWARF kept
alongside the hunk binary you actually run: build with an m68k ELF
toolchain and convert the executable with

(the flow the
amiga-debug VS Code extension uses), then load the ELF’s symbols with
or through the library list below.
Either way the symbols must land at the addresses

query with the load addresses of the
current process’s segment list:
the second, when present. GDB asks

once at attach,
so this relocates a program that is already running: start

from a
CLI inside the emulator (so the process has a

’s symbols at the loaded hunk addresses. When no process seglist
is walkable (ROM code, task rather than process, OS not up yet), the

reply is empty and GDB falls back to link-time addresses --
harmless for ROM-level sessions.

GDB is connected, the stub tracks
the scheduled process’s segment list and reports the load at the moment

's result is installed -- after relocation, before the
program’s first instruction.
The workflow that works with every client,

(gdb) target remote :2345
(gdb) monitor loadseg-break
(gdb) continue
loadseg: hello first hunk $018FE8 (monitor segments / add-symbol-file FILE 0x18FE8)
Program received signal SIGTRAP, Trace/breakpoint trap.
(gdb) disconnect
(gdb) file hello
(gdb) target remote :2345
(gdb) break main
(gdb) continue

stop, with a console hint
naming the program and its first hunk, whenever a new program is
loaded. Detaching does not end the emulator: the machine stays paused
and the stub listens for the next connection, so reattaching with the
program’s file loaded re-runs

against the now-current
process and relocates the symbols with no manual addresses (GDB’s
command ends the server). Alternatively stay attached and use
prints the full hunk map, and the

console command
prints the same map pre-formatted with that hint.

Clients that request GDB’s target library lists get automatic load
events instead: fetching

once arms them, and
each new program then reports a

stop event -- GDB re-reads
the stub’s library list (one

per hunk, named by the command’s basename), relocates a
matching file found via

, binds pending
breakpoints, and resumes on its own. Be aware that stock bare-metal
m68k GDB builds --

alike -- never request library lists, so they never see these events;
use the
CPU PC/SR, frame, beam, instruction position, reverse status
step over a BSR/JSR/TRAP call (single step otherwise)
run until the current subroutine returns to its caller
stop on CPU or Copper writes to the custom register
toggle a stop when the beam reaches that position
toggle a stop when the Copper PC reaches ADDR
reverse-search the last write to a word
the current process’s loaded hunks (LoadSeg addresses)
toggle a visible stop whenever a new program is loaded
tracked program loads with their hunk addresses
